attributes.stem: A 57-year-old man presents with a six-week history of anorexia, weight loss and more recently, persistent vomiting. He brings back material containing recognisable food particles eaten the previous day. He is a non-smoker and drinks two glasses of wine a day. His medical history includes a bleeding duodenal ulcer treated endoscopically some ten years ago. On examination his blood pressure is 110/70 mmHg, pulse 90/min and temperature 37.2oC. His abdomen is distended, but soft. A succussion splash can be produced. A CT scan is performed (Image).
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Carcinoma of the head of the pancreas
attributes.explanation:
Vomiting up of recognisable food particles many hours after the meal suggests gastric stasis. The presence of a succussion splash is supportive of the diagnosis of gastric outlet obstruction and a distended stomach, full of fluid. This physical sign describes the sloshing sound heard when the abdomen is shaken from side to side. The sound is due to the large volume of fluid in the stomach splashing about when shaken. All the conditions listed above could potentially cause gastric outlet obstruction. The weight loss and anorexia leans towards a diagnosis of malignancy.

The CT scan provides further information. It shows a grossly dilated stomach with a stenosing tumour in the pre-pyloric region. This patient almost certainly has a carcinoma of the stomach. Anorexia and weight loss are characteristic features associated with gastric cancer. A succussion splash is a relatively uncommon physical sign.

Chronic duodenal ulcer disease is characterised by a gnawing or burning sensation and that occurs several hours after meal. These patients often have a history of chronic NSAID use or Helicobacter pylori. Pyloric stenosis secondary to peptic ulcer disease is now very uncommon due to the widespread use of proton pump inhibitors.

Superior mesenteric artery syndrome is an uncommon condition characterised by compression of the third, or transverse, portion of the duodenum between the aorta and the superior mesenteric artery. Symptoms can include early satiety, nausea, vomiting, abdominal pain and indigestion. The symptoms tend to be chronic, rather that presenting as progressively as in this case. CT findings that may support a diagnosis of superior mesenteric artery syndrome include a narrowed or compressed duodenum and dilation of the proximal small intestine. An abdominal CT scan may show a "string sign" which is a linear filling defect caused by the compressed duodenum.

Gastric volvulus is a condition in which the stomach twists on its axis, leading to obstruction of the stomach outlet and difficulty in passing food or gas. This can cause symptoms such as nausea, vomiting, abdominal pain and distension, and weight loss. A plain abdominal X-ray may show a "double bubble" sign, which is caused by the stomach and duodenum being twisted. A CT scan may show a twisted stomach with a distal obstruction.

The symptoms typically associated with carcinoma of the head of the pancreas anorexia, abdominal and back pain, weight loss, and jaundice. Duodenal obstruction leading to gastric outlet obstruction is relatively uncommon. The CT findings that would support a diagnosis of carcinoma of the head of the pancreas include a mass in the head of the pancreas, dilatation of the bile duct and invasion of surrounding structures.
learningPoints: The presence of a succussion splash implies gastric outlet obstruction.
references: Mukkamalla SKR, Recio-Boiles A, Babiker HM. Gastric Cancer. [Updated 2022 Jul 10]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2022 Jan-.
